comments.title: Concerning Gwynfydd and higher planes
comments.content:
Archdruid Greer,
I hope all is well with you and Sara.
1) Based upon occult writings as you understand them, once we reach Gwynfydd and begin living the luminous life, will consider ourselves as still living upon the Earth? (My understanding of the higher planes seems to suggest a sort of phase difference, not necessarily a location difference.)
2) Do the planets as we understand them in a scientific way exist on other planes as well (which might imply that the gods live here on earth as well – though upon a higher plane- or is creation completely different on the higher planes?
Regards,
Barry

replies.title: Re: Concerning Gwynfydd and higher planes
replies.content:
1) Yes, but on a different plane, so it may not look much like the earth you're used to.

2) Every planet has a full set of bodies on each of the planes, so that (for example) there is an etheric Mars, an astral Mars, and so on. As for the gods, it depends on which gods you have in mind! The Pagan Neoplatonists, who made a very close study of this topic, noted that there were some gods who definitely existed on earth -- Pan is an obvious example; others, such as Mars, had their primary existence on other worlds in this solar system and influenced the Earth by way of attendant spirits or daimons. Still other gods were entirely outside space and time as we know them and thus were effectively everywhere at once. So it's complex.

replies.title: Re: Concerning Gwynfydd and higher planes
replies.content:
Not the OP, but:

1) Do you mean different plane in the physical/etheric/astral plane sense, or something different?

replies.title: Re: Concerning Gwynfydd and higher planes
replies.content: In the physical/etheric/astral plane sense, or perhaps in the sense of sub-planes within each of those. What's sometimes called the body of light, which is the body inhabited by dwellers in gwynfydd, does not seem to be a body of ordinary dense matter.

replies.title: Re: Concerning Gwynfydd and higher planes
replies.content: Your second answer reminded me of an issue that I've had: trying to understand Neoplatonic philosophy and theology.  I tried reading "Essays on a Polytheistic Philosophy of Religion" by Edward Butler but, outside of the section on interpretation of myth, I got lost in the technical language.  I'm quite unfamiliar with Neoplatonism.  Can you recommend a good book, or other intro, to Neoplatonism, especially as it relates to the Gods?  Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Concerning Gwynfydd and higher planes
replies.content: I found Gregory Shaw's Theurgy and the Soul a good intro, and that should be followed promptly with On the Mysteries by Iamblichus. I wish I knew of a really solid intro to Pagan Neoplatonism for beginners, though.

replies.title: Re: Concerning Gwynfydd and higher planes
replies.content: Might you write this book?

replies.title: Re: Concerning Gwynfydd and higher planes
replies.content: I'd need five to ten spare years to focus on studying the classical Neoplatonists. If that happens, sure.

replies.title: Re: Concerning Gwynfydd and higher planes
replies.content:

I would also recommend our host's article on The One and the many in the first of the Ascendant volumes (https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/48130.html)

Manuel

replies.title: Re: Concerning Gwynfydd and higher planes
replies.content: Thanks for the suggestion, I'll check it out.

replies.title: Re: Concerning Gwynfydd and higher planes
replies.content:
If you like listening to youtube videos, I found this lecture series from Pierre Grimes extremely helpful:

https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCwpMRCoVgSJ-rKyV1yhWljg

The channel has material from the early 80s through to the present day. Much of it is not very good quality, but all of the material from the 1990s are very clear and very listenable. It includes lectures on many of Plato's important dialogs as well as detailed discussions of Proclus and Plotinus.

For me personally, the real challenge with Platonic literature was the vocabulary. Words simply don't mean what they do in common English. "Participation," "energy," "being," "intellect," "essence" and so on all have specialized meanings. If you pair some of Grimes's lectures with reading the material he's discussing, it will help a great deal.

replies.title: Re: Concerning Gwynfydd and higher planes
replies.content: This was actually one of the key problems that I had: understanding the vocabulary.  As I attempted to read Butler's essays, for example, I quickly realized that I didn't understand what he meant by the many technical terms he was using.  Perhaps some kind of Neoplatonic dictionary would be helpful.

Language is a function of meaning , which is in part a function of the mental plane that manifests itself in the plane most of us have fully integrated as astral phenomena —words and sentences. You're also right that our capacities on the mental plane are still pretty minimal.

replies.title: Re: Concerning Gwynfydd and higher planes
replies.content: Single-celled beings are in the same category as most plants -- they have etheric bodies but haven't yet developed an astral sheath. Crystals are elemental beings with an etheric sheath.

replies.title: unlucky month for marriage
replies.content:
Robert Graves has this to say in Chapter Ten of The White Goddess. "H for Uath" "The sixth tree is the whitethorn or hawthorn or may, which takes its name from the month of May. It is, in general, an unlucky tree . . . In ancient Greece, as in Britain, this was the month in which people went about in old clothes . . . They also abstained from sexual intercourse--a custom which explains May as an unlucky month for marriage. . . The Greek Goddess Maia . . . was in fact the White Goddess, who under the name of Cardea . . . cast spells with the hawthorn. The Greeks propitiated her at marriages . . .with five torches of hawthorn-wood and with hawthorn blossom before the unlucky month began."

There is a severely pruned hawthorn bush at the place where I live. It has attractive small leaves and blooms with small white blossoms in early spring. It also has long and very sharp thorns.

The ancient Greek calendar, like the Jewish one, fits lunar months into a solar year, which means that the dates of the Greek equivalent to the month of May shift back and forth according to the Metonic cycle. If taking Graves' advice, it probably would make sense to avoid getting married in the month of May of the common civil calendar.

comments.title: Aether
comments.content:
Eloptic energy, plasma, orgone, the life force, Odic force - are they the same? If not, how do they differ? If so, why are there so many different kinds of devices of wildly differing designs to study and utilize the same energy? What common thread binds a cloud buster to a radionic amplifying pattern printed on a sheet of paper to a magnetically polarized iron model of the earth? It seems to me that to design and make something useful, one needs an understanding of the governing principles involved. How can one ascertain what they are?

I very much enjoy the idea of making practical use of forms of energy that aren't supposed to exist. It's hard for the man to meter them when science furiously insists they aren't there.

Umber Amphibious Pangolin

replies.title: Re: Aether
replies.content:
They're all the same thing -- or at least they all behave in the same way and can do the same things, and experimentation by people who have learned how to work with more than one very strongly suggests that they're all the same.

Why do the devices differ? Well, how many different things do you have in your home that all use electricity? A flashlight, a wall clock, and a battery operated hamster in a martial arts outfit who swings a nunchaku and sings "Kung Fu Fighting" (to name some examples in my home) don't have a lot in common, but they're all powered by electricity, and indeed use the same batteries.

As for the governing principles, we don't know them yet. That's just it. What we have are a handful of devices, techniques, practices, and methods that all seem to work with the same energy. Most of them were discovered by accident while people were trying to do something different. Most radionics technologies were worked out by people who thought they were using electricity, and then figured out that something really odd was going on. The East Asian techniques using qi, similarly, were worked out by people who were trying to figure out how to fight or heal wounds, and discovered that something really odd was going on. We're all venturing into unexplored wilderness, and every so often our trails cross...

comments.title: The stars incline, but do not compel
comments.content:
JMG,

Many times you have used the expression that "The stars incline, but do not compel". And you have written of how stellar inclinations come from the level of the astral plane, and affect everything downstream; but that it is possible to change such inclinations by accessing the mental plane, as it is upstream from the astral plane. (I hope I have summarized correctly.)

I’m hoping you can help me to be sure that I understand what this means, exactly.

(1) As for the exact method of how to change one's fate via the mental plane, is this primarily a matter of building higher capacities for thought and will? So as to be able to think outside of the normal patterns of inclination, and from that to make different choices than the preset patterns, and the will power to actually follow through on them.

(2) Even if one learns to rise beyond the astral tides in one’s own conduct, it seems to me that the astral tides would still continuously create conditions which throws the challenges of their own birth chart at them until the day they die. In other words, even if you build an olympic level body for swimming against the astral tides, the chart you were born with still determine the tides you will forever swim in. Overcoming the inclinations of your natural inborn rhythms will always take directed effort. Do I have the right idea about this?

(3) Would prayer to deities, who exist on or above the mental plane, also potentially be an effective method for overcoming stellar inclination? And if so, is it probably generally more effective to pray for aid in changing oneself, rather than praying for changes to chart-related challenges that come from outside one's self?

replies.title: Re: The stars incline, but do not compel
replies.content:
1) Human beings have very limited access to the mental plane. We don't have mental bodies yet, just mental sheaths, which are structures of mental plane substance evolving toward the status of mental bodies. Any practice that focuses attention on the mental plane and builds the mental sheath -- meditation is of course the most important of these -- helps improve that situation, and gives you a little more capacity to rise above the inclinations of the stars.

2) That's correct. The most you can do is establish habits of consciousness that reliably counteract the inclinations of your natal horoscope.

3) It can be, but it depends on the deity. While deities exist on higher planes, many of them habitually act through planes lower down; the deities who give their names to the planets (and their equivalents in other systems), for example, work on the astral and so may not be a good choice. The Neoplatonists talked about the difference between encosmic and hypercosmic deities -- the encosmic ones are the ones on whose acts the planetary influences are founded, and you can petition them to work with the planetary influences but not to overcome those influences, while the hypercosmic deities are the ones who exist beyond the cosmos as we experience it and can assist in transcendence. (Some of the Gnostics called those the archons and the aeons respectively).

comments.title: Divination advice
comments.content:
Hello again, Mr Greer

Now that you have explained in more grounded terms what magic is about, I don't see it as foolishness anymore, and am becoming in fact interested. Since you prescribe a daily ritual, study and divination, I thought the last part must be the hardest for me, for several reasons. First, I don't believe in the woo woo part of this art. Even if it worked, I really don't want to know the future, I guess if all this stuff was true, then I would have zero agency in my life and I just can't accept this. But I could accept a more grounded version of what divination entails: sharpening human senses and intuitions. Some things require study to understand, some others require a bit of intuition to discover.

My mother is a Tarot practitioner, I never wanted a reading. In fact, I despised her hobby for a long time. Now I accept that she might be helping some lost souls to feel more confident about life, maybe she is giving good advice to her clients, but knowing my mother, I doubt it. Perhaps other readers are sage or psychological enough to give a good advice from what they read in cards. My father is even worse, but he is not into divination or magical practices, he is rather a mystical that pursues any religion belief out there, actually pursuing the monastic life of excess. He's the one that dreams with becoming a pure energy form, free from flesh. Oh, and he hates druids, I don't know why. My wife and I, on the other hand, are quite the rationalistics. We tolerate other people's beliefs but we are non believers. So that's the background.

From what I gather about divination, it is a tool to awaken our intuitions, to let our minds speak freely and to interpret what it is saying. I don't want to scare my wife, so I'm looking for something that I could work in secrecy, maybe when she's out or sleeping. Minimum to none parafernalia.
I also have a grudge against random methods. You know roleplay games, right? When it comes to throwing dices, I always lose in the things that matter, even when my chances are high. If it is card games, it is the same. The more luck is required, the more consistently I lose. I have dices from my previous gamer life and my wife would not suspect about them, but I don't feel like using them.

This weekend I went through a list of divination methods, and the one that called up my attention was scrying. When I was meditating (eastern style) images were common, and I had to dispell them as we were told to do. So I think that's a way I could let my mind express itself. It's silent, can be practiced at night and I have the confidence that I can obtain results. It also fits well with my beliefs. So I did try.

The first attempt went badly. I was interrupted several times and when I finally had some peaceful time, my heart strangely started beating like crazy. I wasn't nervous, I wasn't seeing anything yet, but I had to stop. Maybe I didn't feel nervous, but apparently something inside me was.
Yesterday, my second attemp went better. I didn't see anything clearly, just a few flashes, the same you see when you close your eyes hard. But I suspected the shape of an eye, or a vagina, then something like a sofa, the window of a house seen from outside and a comet. My wife and I usually have sex on sundays, but when I met my wife in the sitting room, she was sat on the sofa, reading a book in our ebook reader and I knew she was not in the mood. She confirmed what I thought.

I was expecting some symbolism, but these images were quite explicit, actually. Dark, blurry, just a hint for shapes, but the shapes themselves were not very symbolic. So sex with my wife (vagina?) who was sitting on the sofa will be rejected (house seen from outside?) and go bad (the comet omen?). If I wanted a proof that it works, I couldn't ask for a better one. I think that somehow my mind already knew what was going to happen from hints I had during the evening, and that was intuition showing.

I still have some doubts, but they are more about the current practice. I have used my smartphone in black as background, seeing the reflect of a dim light in the ceiling. I understand that using a device which is used for other purposes might be loaded with influences. In other words, I might feel tempted to read my WhatsApp messages, and that leads to think about my WhatsApp contacts, driving my thoughts in directions I don't want to follow. However, I'm becoming pretty good at ignoring my smartphone when I don't want to be in contact, so it might not be such a major influence. I could try other focusing tool, but that one is very convinient.
Another doubt is about where to sit the sight. It's hard to keep the sight in the glaced surface. It's easier to focus on the reflected wall or ceiling but since it has no features, the easiest thing to do is to unfocus the sight, looking at the infinite. When unfocused, everything is blurry, the eyes feel better, and images still come. But then, what's the point on using a glaced surface if I could just look at a blank wall instead with the same results? And when I fix the focus on the surface, in three minutes I stop seeing the device, it becomes a black area once the rods and cones are saturated.
I will keep trying to rest the sight in different foci to see what goes better.

Is there any advice you could offer here?
Thanks in advance.

replies.title: Re: Divination advice
replies.content:
If I may chime in... you wrote, "It's silent, can be practiced at night and I have the confidence that I can obtain results."

If you want to test whether or not humans can know the future, another method that works with what you wrote is, dream work. Just keep a record of your dreams for awhile and sooner or later you'll notice you have at times precognitive dreams. I think everyone has precognitive dreams from time to time, but we just don't always know it, because most people don't remember their dreams unless they make it a habit and give it practice.

Dreams are natural to everyone and paying attention to them is a surefire way to develop: creativity, intuition, imagination, and so much more.

Here is a little post on precognitive dreams from Robert Moss, one of the better teachers of dreaming arts out there IMO:

http://mossdreams.blogspot.com/2017/09/we-dream-future-all-time.html

Just some thoughts!

Tangerine Orthogonal Wombat

replies.title: Re: Divination advice
replies.content:
Thank you.
I thought about this too, but I don't sleep too much and must wake up quite early, so I can't wake me up earlier to keep up track of my dreams. It's a pity, since they are lively.

replies.title: Re: Divination advice
replies.content:
First of all, if you're comfortable with scrying, use scrying. Jung called it "active imagination," and used it as a source of psychological insight. You can do the same.

Second, a blank wall is just fine. You don't have to use a glazed surface. You don't have to use any surface at all. I scry by unfocusing my eyes and paying attention to the images that arise in my imagination -- that's the standard Golden Dawn method. It can be done in perfect secrecy and requires no paraphernalia at all, so meets your criteria.

Third, you can't "know the future" in the sense of knowing everything that's going to happen before it happens. What you can do is catch glimpses of where certain chains of cause and effect are tending -- and if it's something that depends on your actions, you can change what you do and make a different future. That's one of the points of practicing divination -- it allows you to look ahead, see problems in the distance, and avoid them.

replies.title: Re: Divination advice
replies.content:
Many thanks.

The blank wall and the unfocused sight responds my current doubts, and I think this will work better than the glazed surface, which is small and uncomfortable. Also, no weird influences.
I will try to keep a diary with the images I see and what I think these might mean.

Today I saw clearly a sword, then something that looked like a starting fire, then something that looked like burning smoke. No idea yet of what it means, if it means anything. Maybe it's not related to anything that has passed today.
Am I right in thinking that the images meaning is personal? That whatever a sword means for me is not the same as for anyone else?

replies.title: Re: Divination advice
replies.content: Yes, that's quite correct. You can work out the meanings by meditation, or by journaling: ask yourself "what does X mean?" and then write down whatever comes to mind, without censoring or editing any of it. The result will often be a jumble but if you think about what you get, the answer will very often become clear.

replies.title: Re: Etheric charging
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Joining OBOD

I don't know if this'll help any, but most poetry did absolutely nothing for me for the first forty years of my life. I could always make out a Robert Frost poem: he wrote about things that made sense, also they were things, as a country girl, I had experienced, nearly enough, that I knew what he was talking about.

At forty, suddenly poetry made sense to read. It's really quite weird: I still have the old aversion, but when I do read some it works, even though I still expect it to just be words. It has nothing to do with reading speed: I still read very fast and never hear words in my head while reading.

Off course I'd studied it in college lit, but it never (except Frost and later Emily Dickensen) had any meaning beyond words on a page. I managed, back in the day when thinking actually mattered in academia, to disagree well enough on poetry with the professor to earn As on the essays on poems, but I didn't understand them.

If I live long enough, perhaps in another forty years I'll finally be able to write poetry!

comments.title: Organizing Long Term Magical Studies (When You are ADD)
comments.content:
HI JMG and greetings to the commentariat.

Your post on radionics touched something in me and tugged on a thread of interest I realize has been brewing inside of me for quite awhile. In my late teens and early twenties I started reading Wilhelm Reich and got into that material for quite awhile. Then a little later an older gentleman I knew very briefly before he passed turned me on to the work of Philip Callahan. Through the interest in Callahan's work I started getting interested in "Natural Radio" -the natural emissions of the Earth in the VLF range of the spectrum. At the time I was doing community radio at WAIF, so radio in itself was an interest -and from there wanted to know more about shortwave & ham radio, eventually getting involved in these latter two activities, many years later.

About a year or so ago I was looking into Earth Pipe's out of curiosity, and found a site (that I can't seem to find again!) that was a Radionics / Orgone / alt-science research group or organization of some kind. I realized as someone who most likely is on the ADD/ADHD spectrum that though it was calling to me at that time, I had other projects I wanted to see to completion and other goals I wanted to achieve.

Again your post on Radionics really did tug that thread and fire up my imagination, recalling these areas of interest and your own research as documented in The Secret of the Temple.

In any case, the point here is that I do want to study Radionics in more depth. Some kind of correspondence course or program of self-study I could follow would be what I need to pursue this. I have some ideas of readings / re-readings I could follow up on.

My question though, is how have you decided amidst all the aspects of occultism you have studied and all the courses you have done, what to pursue next? I remember you said that there was a toss up for a time of whether you would pursue alchemy or astrology as a serious area of work. You did an alchemy course and realized that your talents might be better put to use elsewhere -and your astrology has bloomed.

I can think of a few things to guide me on what I will study and work on in more detail next: inner guidance / journaling, meditation, divination. replies.title: Re: Organizing Long Term Magical Studies (When You are ADD)
replies.content:
I don't have a simple formula for this, and if I did, it might not work for other people; I have the weird combination of Aspergers syndrome and mild ADD. What that means in practice is that I go zooming off on new enthusiasms fairly often, spend a few weeks or months or maybe a couple of years wallowing in them, and then usually go on to something else. It's when one of those enthusiasms turns out to be enduring that I make it a part of my daily life. Occultism is one of those -- my enthusiasm for that dates back to roughly thirty seconds after I found out that magic wasn't just something in fantasy novels -- and there are various others, which I fit into my schedule all anyhow.

The alchemy/astrology thing is a good example. I decided to give alchemy a fair trial, spent a couple of years getting deep into spagyrics, felt the familiar waning of interest, and then tried astrology instead. In that case the interest didn't wane, though it varies in intensity. I also sometimes take something up, set it aside, then come back to it later, sometimes more than once.

replies.title: Re: intercepted houses
replies.content: The sign on the cusp is the lord of the house, full stop, end of sentence. It doesn't matter whether there's just a sliver of the sign in the house -- the sign on the cusp is the lord of the house, and the planet that rules the sign is the ruler of the house. Thus there are no "partial interceptions" -- if a cusp falls in any part of a sign, that sign is not intercepted.

replies.title: Re: Weird New England
replies.content: Gage -- hmm. Any chance you're related to Matilda Joslyn Gage? She was quite active in the occult scene, among many other places. Beyond that, once Robert Matheisen makes a comment, message him -- he used to teach university courses about occultism in American history and can point you to some serious sources of data.

replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Weird New England
replies.content: She was a very important American Theosophist. She was also the mother-in-law of L. Frank Baum, the author of The Wizard of Oz, and she was probably the model for Glinda the Good Witch.

replies.title: Re: Weird New England
replies.content:
If your ancestor came with the Winthrop Fleet, then he is most likely John Gage. He settled in Boston in 1630, but he left Boston for Ipswich in 1638, and then left Ipswich for Bradford in 1662. He was an early member of the Boston Puritan Church, and eventually transferred his membership to the Puritan Church at Ipswich. There are four pages about him in Robert Charles Anderson's The Great MIgration Begins (1995), pp. 719-722. There's no indication, alas, that he had any "unsanctioned" interests.

Gage is Matilda Joslyn's married surname; her husband was Henry Hill Gage. He seems to have been a great-great-great grandson of one Thomas Gage, who lived in Yarmouth, out on Cape Cod, where he married Johanna Knight in 1647. He is said to have come to New England with the Puritan minister Ezekiel Rogers around 1638 on the ship "John of London," but left the most settled parts of Massachusetts for the Cape, where he may have earned his keep at sea (wehether as a fisherman or a sailor seems unclear).

The two Gage families seem not to be related, or perhaps they were only very distantly related. A quick genealogical search doesn't reveal as much reliable documentation for Thomas as for John. And of course you need to check whether your Gage ancestors stem from John or from Thomas. Have you got good family records for that?

There isn't a lot of really good information available on the actual practice of magic and divination in Massachusetts in the 1600s. There's more from the 1700s and a lot more from the 1800s. However, it's generally known that such activities flourished in seaports, probably due to the great risks of trying to earn a living at sea. And sailors in New England appear to have been much less inclined to become formal members of any church than were men who earned their living off the land.

replies.title: Re: canonized or not
replies.content:
In THE Orthodox Church, it's pretty much understood that people who are on the calendar with "Saint" in front of their names are the ones officially recognized, but by no means the only ones who matter. Some, like John Chrysostom or Nicholas of Myra, are important to the whole Church; others, like Herman of Alaska, Raphael of Brooklyn, or Alexis of Wilkes-Barre, are important to a specific region - these three were important in the establishment of Orthodoxy in North America, for instance.

There are also some eccentric ones like the "fools for Christ" - essentially, nutty people or street people, shunned by respectable folk, who were nevertheless touched by the Holy Spirit, and who gained a reputation among common people for giving good spiritual advice. (An interesting one is Xenia of St. Petersburg, who lost her mind after her soldier husband died and became what we would call a "bag lady", but collected a group of followers anyway, and was eventually canonized by the Moscow Patriarchate.) This attitude goes right down the line, to people never recognized by any Church authority, but who are prayed to as saints by families or individuals, because in their lives they were important spiritual beacons; and after death, prayers offered to them seem to be answered. John Coltraine would certainly be an eccentric choice, but if the Spirit moves, and praying to him gets results, why would he be less a saint than, say, Andrew the Fool For Christ of Constantinople? :D Though I would add: God will understand, but I'm not so sure about the pastor! :)

An interesting study was done quite a few years ago about how people process fears. The researchers interviewed people who lived downstream from a big dam that wasn't in good shape. They found that as they got closer and closer to the dam, people got more stressed out about it...until they got within sight of the dam, and the people there insisted that they weren't afraid at all, that of course the dam would never break, and so on.

The same thing is going on here. I don't know whether telekinesis is accomplished by an extension of the etheric body or with something more subtle. I have no mastery of this and have barely fooled around with it, but like most people who are interested in magic, I have wondered about it.

Usually telekinesis tricks are aimed not at living things but things conventionally thought to be inanimate, like a candle flame or a spoon.

Chucking energy across a thirty foot circle at someone you are facing, and having them react to it, takes practice but you don't have to be a great adept, because it's going from your etheric body to theirs, with a knock-on effect on their physical nervous system.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) "Analogous" is an understatement. The AODA equinox and solstice rituals were developed out of the Golden Dawn equinox rituals in a very straightforward manner, probably (though I'm not sure of this) by Juliet Ashley, the third Grand Archdruid, who was also a Golden Dawn initiate. The main difference is that the AODA rituals use a specifically astrological framework -- star, sun, moon, and earth -- rather than the Cabalistic form of the pillar. Of course there are broader applications -- or there can be, if you choose to create them.

2) At most, something like that could apply only within a specific system or set of systems. Magical rituals are *extremely* varied and follow a galaxy of different principles and structures, and I don't think anybody could study enough magical systems in a lifetime to get any kind of broad overview. All my magical writings are shaped by the two magical systems I've studied in depth -- the Golden Dawn system on the one hand, and the AODA/UGF/related orders system on the other -- and by some limited triangulation between those; beyond that, I'm not qualified to go.

replies.content: No, what happened was that Juliet Ashley -- the third Grand Archdruid of AODA, and the founder of a bunch of other orders related to it -- stopped in England in 1939 on the way home from studying psychology with Carl Jung in Zurich, and visited Arthur Edward Waite. According to what I was taught, he gave her a charter for an American branch of his Golden Dawn offshoot, the Holy Order of the Golden Dawn, and a few years later she launched it in Philadelphia, where she was then living. Since it was in the Waite lineage, it didn't teach ceremonial magic -- Ashley's focus was spiritual development, anyway, rather than thaumaturgy -- but the tradition is compatible with the usual Golden Dawn ritual magic.

comments.title: Healing a weather radio, maybe
comments.content:
Recently I woke up to find that the display of the radio that I use to receive the broadcasts from the local weather service office had gone dark. However, when I punched the button to receive the weather broadcast, it was present as usual. The radio also has an alarm function, which worked as usual. I prefer its alarm to using the cell phone's alarm function. Without the display, I would still be notified of severe weather watches and warnings, which is the most important function of the radio (I can't get those on my flip phone) but I could not use the radio as a clock. I didn't know what to make of a nonfunctional display but a working radio function. The bedside lamp worked when I turned it on, so the receptacle was receiving electricity.

Not knowing what else to try (the only user serviceable part of the radio is to replace the batteries, which should not have been an issue because the radio was plugged in), I decided I would use the Secret Work of the Grail from Grade 7 of the DA course to bless and fix the display of the weather radio. I've used it to bless and heal plants, but this was the first time I tried it on an electronic device. Using "bless and fix the display of the weather radio" as the intention, I proceeded as if I were enchanting a stone, except that rather than hold the radio in my hands, I held my hands an inch or two away and directed the lunar current into the radio. Nothing changed at that point. After the ritual was over I plugged the radio into a different receptacle. Nothing changed.

After I finished the rest of my daily practices, I returned to the radio. Unplugged but with the batteries in, the weather radio broadcast came on when I pushed the appropriate button, but the display remained dark. So I decided to change the batteries, to ground the ritual in the material plane. I tested each battery when I took it out. All tested low but not low enough to replace. Upon replacing the batteries with new batteries, the display came on.

Maybe it was a coincidence. Maybe it was more than that. Have you done magic similar to this with electronic devices? If so, have you had success with it? I seem to have an easier time with understanding how enchanting a stone can work compared with an electronic device, but that might just be a cultural artifact.

replies.title: Re: Healing a weather radio, maybe
replies.content:
Hmmm. The clock-display shutoff function is a 'failsafe' that is intended to keep the weather alert running as long as possible, by sacrificing the clock display, when the unit is running off battery.

How old is the unit? Sometimes tiny little wires, or the soldered connections, can crack and fail over time, simply from age.

It sounds like the unit is 'confused' about whether or not the power is on. That might also account for the batteries being low- its been running off them part of the time, when it didn't need to.

replies.title: Re: Healing a weather radio, maybe
replies.content:
About the shutoff function being a failsafe, that makes sense, and thank you; I didn't know that. The unit is only supposed to be on battery power when the electricity isn't on, which is quite rare, usually only when we are in the basement waiting out a tornado warning. We've had more reliable electricity than usual in the last year or two so I didn't think the batteries were ready to be changed, though it's entirely possible that they were. I didn't notice if there was a low battery warning on the display before it failed; there may have been.

I'm not sure how old the unit is. Perhaps five or so years old. It's also quite possible that it is failing from age, but annoying if so, because I am old enough to have had weather radios that lasted much longer. Another sign of decline ... grrrr ...

replies.title: Re: Healing a weather radio, maybe
replies.content: I've found that a banishing ritual can sometimes make a balky internet connection work, but I've never used it to fix a clock radio. Still, interesting.

comments.content:
You could do worse than start by drawing a map of Oz with your children, with the four lands of Oz aligned to the four points of the compass, and the Emerald City at the center. Then think--and get your children to think--about why each land might have the color that Baum said it had, and how these might relate to the standard color wheel of three primary colors and three secondary colors.

Then try assigning the classic four elements to the four compass points. There are two really ancient ways of doing this, the celestial (or astrological) and the terrestrial (or alchemical). The terrestrial way puts fire in the south and water in the north; earth therefore is in the west and air in the east. You ascend the ladder of the elements by moving clockwise around the circle; you descend that ladder by moving counterclockwise. In the celestial way water and earth trade places, which causes part of the circumference to cross over itself as the elements move. Which of the two did Baum seem to prefer, or does his colored map have elements of both?

Superimpose the terrestrial and celestial patterns, one on the other, and you will get a circle with a cross, which is a very, very ancient cosmogram known from all over the world, including in some branches of unwritten African traditional cosmological lore.

And then speculate why the good witches are in the north and south, the bad ones in the east and west--and the magnificent charlatan is found at the center of them all. And why the Deadly Desert surrounds them all, rather like Dion Fortune's Ring-Pass-Not.

By the time you've done all this with your children, you'll all be well on the way to developing a complete esoteric analysis of Baum's world of Oz, which may also be an esoteric analysis of our own world from Baum's angle of vision.

The later, post-Baum Oz books may not turn out to adhere to Baum's cosmic vision. That will depend on how well the later authors understood what Baum was up to.

For a graduate degree in all this, work on correlating Baum's model of the cosmos with Blavatsky's.

comments.title: Scottish pantheon?
comments.content:
I have noticed that when you speak of the Celtic pantheon, you divide it into Irish and Welsh.

Did the Celts not penetrate as far north as Scotland? Did the Picts have their own pantheon, that we just don't know much about?

I know that later, when the Viking diaspora began, the northern islands and the coastal regions fell, so did the Norse pantheon colonize there instead?

replies.title: Re: Scottish pantheon?
replies.content: It's rather more complex than that. The Irish and the Welsh are the two pantheons for which we have any significant amount of written evidence. The Picts were a mix of peoples, some Celtic, some pre-Celtic; the Celtic Picts were related to the Welsh; but in the early Dark Ages what is now Scotland was conquered by invaders from Ireland, from whom most of later Scottish culture descends. So the Irish pantheon was also the pantheon of the ancestors of the Scots.
